title: Why the Schrodinger Equation Is a Theorem, Not a Postulate

metadata.color_law: TEAL = the derivation steps (probability conservation -> unitarity -> Hermitian generator -> exponential -> Schrodinger eq); CRIMSON = the postulate framing being displaced
metadata.note: Exclusions: no interaction-picture detour, no path-integral derivation, no Hamiltonian constraint in curved spacetime, no Stone's theorem
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B14: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Every textbook presents the Schrodinger equation as a fundamental axiom — a thing we just accept because it works. One physical requirement makes it unavoidable.

card.title: Why the Schrodinger Equation Is a Theorem, Not a Postulate

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Every textbook presents the Schrodinger equation as a fundamental axiom — a thing we just accept because it works. One physical requi

beats.act: COLD OPEN — probabilities must sum to one
beats.narration_text: Start with one physical fact: the total probability of finding a particle somewhere must always equal one, no matter how the state changes in time. That is all you need.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Start with one physical fact: the total probability of finding a particle somewhere must always equal one, no matter how the state changes in time. That is all

beats.act: COLD OPEN — the constraint forces a form
beats.narration_text: That single requirement — probabilities sum to one — constrains time evolution to be a unitary operator. And from unitarity alone, step by step, the Schrodinger equation falls out.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: That single requirement — probabilities sum to one — constrains time evolution to be a unitary operator. And from unitarity alone, step by step, the Schrodinger

beats.narration_text: If probabilities must always sum to one, what does that force time evolution to look like? Does it really force the exact equation textbooks call a postulate?

card.title: Does probability conservation force the Schrodinger equation?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: If probabilities must always sum to one, what does that force time evolution to look like? Does it really force the exact equation textbooks call a postulate?

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — postulate framing hides the chain
beats.narration_text: When you write the Schrodinger equation as a postulate, you hide a derivable chain. You could just as easily have postulated the wrong equation. The constraint actually picks out the right one.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: When you write the Schrodinger equation as a postulate, you hide a derivable chain. You could just as easily have postulated the wrong equation. The constraint

beats.narration_text: Norm must be preserved at all times. The time-evolution operator U must satisfy U-dagger times U equals the identity. That is the definition of a unitary operator.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Norm must be preserved at all times. The time-evolution operator U must satisfy U-dagger times U equals the identity. That is the definition of a unitary operat

beats.narration_text: For an infinitesimal step, unitarity forces the generator to be Hermitian — that means its eigenvalues are real. Those eigenvalues are the energies. Real energies follow from probability conservation.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: For an infinitesimal step, unitarity forces the generator to be Hermitian — that means its eigenvalues are real. Those eigenvalues are the energies. Real energi

beats.narration_text: Compose N infinitesimal unitary steps. As N goes to infinity you get the matrix exponential: U of t equals e to the minus i H t over h-bar. This is the exact time-evolution operator.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Compose N infinitesimal unitary steps. As N goes to infinity you get the matrix exponential: U of t equals e to the minus i H t over h-bar. This is the exact ti

beats.narration_text: Differentiate the exponential with respect to time. The result is exactly i h-bar times d-psi-d-t equals H-psi. The Schrodinger equation is the derivative of unitarity.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Differentiate the exponential with respect to time. The result is exactly i h-bar times d-psi-d-t equals H-psi. The Schrodinger equation is the derivative of un

beats.narration_text: A corollary: because H must be Hermitian, its eigenvalues must be real. That means measured energies are always real numbers — not because we assumed it, but because probabilities must stay between zero and one.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A corollary: because H must be Hermitian, its eigenvalues must be real. That means measured energies are always real numbers — not because we assumed it, but be

beats.narration_text: A student is asked to derive time evolution from scratch, starting only from 'probabilities sum to one.' She writes U-dagger U equals I, extracts the Hermitian generator, composes N steps to get the exponential, and differentiates to find the Schrodinger equation — the equation she thought was given. She notes energies must be real, a fact she never questioned before.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A student is asked to derive time evolution from scratch, starting only from 'probabilities sum to one.' She writes U-dagger U equals I, extracts the Hermitian

beats.narration_text: Probability conservation forces unitarity. Unitarity forces a Hermitian generator. The exponential of a Hermitian generator, differentiated, is the Schrodinger equation. Not a postulate — a theorem.

card.title: Probability conservation forces the Schrodinger equation.
